item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apparently, the image of this article (now deleted) comes from here

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this is old and tbh I should really get on freshening it up one of these days.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it's now 2019, and almost a year since the last comment. though my -1 may not mean much, it's still backed with some feedback:

  • most SCPs make logs like the cross-linked incidents in this one dropdowns instead of actual links to other pages. as others in this comment section have said, having these pages linked makes the article harder to read.
  • the amount of redaction in addendum 001 almost makes it completely useless as an addition to the article. it's not about "leaving it to the imagination", my imagination needs something to go off of.
  • is that reclassification to keter still pending? may want to update something on that to be honest.
  • after incident 027, is the SCP even still contained? is it neutralized? that log seems to indicate that the SCP itself is still out there. if that's the case, the main article should be updated accordingly.

basically, it's one of those S1 SCPs that doesn't quite cut the mustard for me, but the concept is there, and i feel like updating it with just a bit more modern approach would make it better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't get this one. A lot of the information is scattered across several linked pages. It makes it hard to read. I didn't even notice the links when I first read this one, and I fairly lost on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the incident logs make this great.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Hey that's awesome! I'll go ahead and take the timer down.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Looks good to me!

EDIT: I've taken the initiative of dropping in the addendum myself =)

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Came here to recommend SCP-1111 as the most appropriate crosslink. I see sirpudding has already done so. Seconded. Carry on.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Gee wiz sirpudding, how come you get to recommend two skips for crosslinks?

I'm helping!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Ah. Thanks for the catch.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: psst it's 582. 528 is Factory-made silly-putty ripoffs

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Yes, as much as I like coyote yiffmaster, this does seem like a more fitting entry.

Fully support the revised addition

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I'll defer to whatever conclusion you come to, but please bear in mind my tale Mothers' Love. The Foundation doesn't know it in-universe, but SCP-023's true nature is essentially an aggregate grudge curse forced into the shape of a dog to weaken its power. It's much more like SCP-1111 than SCP-2547-1.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Gee wiz sirpudding, how come you get to recommend two skips for crosslinks?

In all seriousness, thanks for pointing out SCP-1111 to me, as it is a much better fit for 023 than 2547-1. That being said, a proposal I would make for such a crosslink would likely look like this:

Addendum 023-004:
Due to both anomalies focusing on specific geographic spaces, their destructive capabilities, and canine appearance, it is possible that SCP-1111-1 may be a variant of the same phenomenon observed in SCP-023, or vice versa. Investigation into the origin of both anomalies is ongoing. Due to the inability to capture SCP-1111-1 for study, investigations are currently focused on SCP-023.

With respect to Ihp's comment, I'm going to hold onto that for when SCP-2547 comes up on the crosslinking list, as SCP-582 would make a very strong candidate for crosslinking with SCP-2547.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
British black dogs are probably more similar to the white dogs of American folklore, and we do have one of those.

Also, Crafter Fredrick, SCP-1913-3, shares some similarities to black dogs, and also personally invented canines during the creation. However I'm not sure if links into Pitch Haven are really a good idea, given the specific assumptions in that canon.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I dunno about this. Black dogs, as you said, associated with the British Isles, while 2547-1 is the Navajo Coyote. Despite what Gunnerkrigg Court might lead one to think, they've got no real association other than they're dogs; one lives in the desert and is worshiped, the other lives in the moors and is feared.

Plus, 2547 is somewhat tragic; Coyote takes over towns with a pack of dogs, yes, but it's about him being forgotten by the world at large, and the Foundation's method of containment beings spreading stories about him. It's kind of a jarring thematic discontinuity.

If anything, provided permission can be obtained, I'd try integrating it into SCP-582. Both of them are about beings kept alive solely through stories; one is just one the Foundation needs to surpress, the other the Foundation needs to keep alive.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Author made edits. Timer removed early. See below for details and discussion.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Given that SCP-023 is, if I remember my folklore correctly, a Black Dog of the folklore of the British Isles, an interesting cross link would be to the other resident folklore based canine found in SCP-2547. As such, I am proposing the following additional addendum:

Addendum 023-004:
Due to several hypotheses that SCP-023 may be associated with SCP-2547-1 or a member of 2547's pack, approval has been given for Foundation personnel to interview SCP-2547-1 about SCP-023 during its next manifestation.

Granted, 023 is most certainly not associated with 2547-1 or its pack, but the potential for the latter to know about the former does generate interesting ideas.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty good. I upvoted it. Good story, but a little morbid. So the dog itself is malicious? Or is it mere coincidence that it suddenly got these powers? Perhaps you could answer that?

item.title: I love it
item.content:encoded: I love it its creepy, cool AND ITS A DOG!!!! I LOVE DOGS!!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Necroposting on my own skip, but, to be fair, this thing has escaped a bunch of times. I'm just perpetually flip-flopping on whether the containment procedures are delicate enough to warrant a Keter. Hence, the containment class will always be "Euclid, Keter pending" to keep me from going in and changing it every eight months.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The first SCP that I ever read, so, very nostalgic, take my upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sounds like it could do a lot of damage.

Item classes aren't based on how dangerous an SCP is, but the difficulty of containing it. A pocket watch with the anomalous ability to kill people at close range for no apparent reason would still be considered Safe class, because you can put it in a box and it won't try to dynamite itself out or something.

Euclid class behaviors are generally not well understood, so if you put them in a box then you're probably not sure if it'd escape or not.

Keter class means you know for sure that unless the box has specific and trustworthy precautions, you know the SCP is going to violently get out and eat your face.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've only seen this page with all of the recent edits proposed in these comments, but based in what I'm reading it has been significantly improved. It's definitely worth my vote now.

But one thing still irks me… Why wouldn't this be classified as Keter? The last addendum mentions that it's being considered for Keter, but seriously, a dog who kills anyone who makes eye contact? Sounds like it could do a lot of damage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every time they tried containing it in a normal containment chamber, it walked out and plopped itself down in a hallway intersection.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Black Dog myth SCP- aww yeah. This is actually rather well executed, although I wish there was some bit going on about how the Foundation figured out the crossroad trick, but that's a personal thing.
+1ed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like this one, incident reports definitely add to it and promote the, "we secure, contain, protect, because otherwise we might f*ck things up even worse!" attitude they have around here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 12 is a nice, round number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(I rather not reply to the Mod Post)

Wikipedia definition here, but considering 1 candela as roughly the same amount of light produced by a regular candle, I'd say any number between 5 to 20 would suffice to describe the state of 023's "eyes" as the rubber starts to degrade, depending on how bright its eye sockets must be in order to effectively manifest the anomaly.

Candela measurement usually requires knowledge about how light is distributed, though: A light bulb, which radiates light in all directions, usually gives out 110 ~ 130 candela, while even the weakest flashlights will likely emit over 1000 candelas, since they work by projecting focused beams.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Weird as hell, but I like it! +1

item.title: Re: Mod Post
item.content:encoded:
Excellent. Thanks!

From chat conversation, Pig_catapult doesn't remember what that redacted number was supposed to be. Does anyone want to suggest anything?

item.title: Re: Mod Post
item.content:encoded: Approved.

item.title: Mod Post
item.content:encoded:
Brightness greater than ██ candela requires that the inserts be replaced within twelve (12) hours.

This really should not be censored. Pig_catapult, will you approve un-redacting this detail?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You must be kidding me. Please don't tell me you think that's where the Black Shuck… you know what, it's fine. It's an obscure enough old British myth, it's fine that you don't know about it. Here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to downvote this comment for failures in spelling

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i Think i saw somethign disturbing similar to this on tv (Animal planet to be more specific). But, i liked enough to downvote, so at the end will be:

+1 because i like it
-1 because the alck of originality

Neutral vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. Redacting information necessary for containment was actually mentioned in one of the how-to's as something to specifically NOT do. However, DrBright is right, if the writer wants it a certain way, then they have the final say. I thought about changing it as well, but changing something that has been redacted or censored is a pretty large change and is up to the writer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you may want to re-read the various newbie guides. It's not uncommon to make that particular mistake early on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, Dr Bright, guess I misunderstood the SOP here. I was thinking a bit more wikipedia. Hope I haven't offended.

Time for me to review how things work, I guess.

After reviewing rules, I see I just drew the line for "minor edits" too high.

I'll learn from my mistake.

Consider this commentary, then: redacting the brightness level makes no sense. I've seen similar complaints on other articles, that deleting, redacting or obfuscating containment procedures is counter to the intent of the memos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it's not your SCP, you don't get to make that choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I changed "[] candela" to "1700 lumens" with the following reasoning:

Redacting information necessary to containment procedures makes no sense, and as such, a specific number is required. Lumens seem to be a more appropriate unit for luminance, at least as far as I can see. 1700 is an arbitrarily-chosen figure, roughly the output of a 25w bulb, according to wikipedia. Seems eyes about that bright might be pushing hard into danger territory.

I think more specific details on the rubber balls is required, even if merely in a footnote or aside "cf model 15-z Baro-Matic hard vulcanized rubber ball (color immaterial)" sort of thing, or a ref to a memo or diagram.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The new picture saddens me a bit… I always imagined this guy as a giant labrador. :P

Oh well, I have a soft spot in my heart for hell hounds of all sorts. Have a much-belated upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I posted something similar in the "Are you a bad enough dude to rescue the SCP" thread, but if you give me a week, I'd love to work on this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love that idea - a rewrite on those lines to reinforce why it is the SCP not D foundation would be better in my opinion. Trim the main article, bring in the incident reports which are far more interesting. Something like

- initially SCP-023 is a completely physical "dog" and people die a year after looking at it.
- Foundation locks it in a box, problem solved.
- Research on the creature is tapped, Site director orders it hobbled and/or killed.
- Whoops, turns out it is an evil spirit that was trapped in the body.
- SCP-023 now appears nightly at a random cross road in an area too large to seal off.
- each night a containment team (who are themselves doomed because they will see the creature) has to search all possible cross roads and set up road blocks, with people dying if they take too long or if 023 appears at a busy locale.
- for added emo-ness the initial containment team is drawn from the researchers who set it free. Either way, assignment to the containment team is a death sentence (D-classes probably not an option given it is out in the wild so to speak).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an idea that I've had for a while, but I have absolutely no idea how to implement it in-article. Help?

Having eyes isn't 023's natural state. It used to be that it could travel great distances during the day, and seeing any part of it caused the eventual death effect. Something else, long before we actually contained it, gave it eyes in an attempt to limit the damage it can do. Then we went and fucked it up by removing them.

item.title: Re: Keter?
item.content:encoded: Because for all we know if we try to kill it it will explode into a thousand copies of its eyes and they'll fly around and kill everyone ever. Really, it's already impossible, why should we expect that killing it will do what we want it to?

item.title: Re: Keter?
item.content:encoded:
Secure. Contain. Protect. Not Destroy.

Unless it IS classified as Keter, it's to be studied. Even then, it's to be studied. Where did it come from? What are it's motivations?

The thing to remember is that there are reasons to study even the most dangerous of things - just because you've caught ONE doesn't mean there's not another out there. Or something that preys upon it.

item.title: Keter?
item.content:encoded: If the thing might be Keter level, why don't they try to kill it? It's obviously vulnerable to damage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm, I won't take credit then. I didn't do that much anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, sophos. He was asking if he should remove his upvote. That was the point.

Ragazzo, if you plan on claiming credit for work on the SCP, you should remove your upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you look at the rating history, it'll tell you that you already upvoted it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can I no longer vote on this since I put in more than a casual edit? I don't think I can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, you know, even I think this one sucks, and it's my SCP.

Please, someone overhaul it. Or delete it. I'm kinda ashamed to even have my name attached to it at this point, even though it technically counts towards my required three SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*adds some stuff*

Is it better now? I'm afraid I've opened up some more plotholes, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-023 just needs a lot more stuff. I don't mean it needs more power, or abilities, but the description section right now is five sentences. Five. That's as many sentences as you've read in this post so far, and I haven't said anything of value.

How does it behave? Why is it at the crossroads? Why did they take out its teeth? Did it bite a bunch of people? If it doesn't need to eat and has magic death eyes, why not fill its eye sockets with titanium replacements, then slap a hood and muzzle on it, and check in on it once a month?

Also, it took me a couple read throughs to notice the actual scary SCP nature of the beast (The killing stare) because it's only one sentence. That should be the main focus of the SCP, not jamming things in its eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright. *fixes that*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a somewhat trifling style tip-

I've been seeing a number of recent SCPs opening their description with "appears to be" or "seems to be". In some cases this is appropriate, such as when there is reason to doubt the SCP's actual physical form, or when the SCP's effects/abnormalities are perception-related.

However, if it's a dog, JUST SAY IT'S A DOG. Even if it's a ~spooky~ dog. And especially if you're going to use the word "apparently" immediately after, in the same sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The part about what happened with glass eyes (in incident 023-27) wasn't in the original myth. >_>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What about now? I've changed Addendum 2 and added some stuff to the paragraph about the eye inserts

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a rewrite of an existing myth, with nothing new or interesting added to it.
